1: wayyaʿan ʾiyyôḇ wayyōʾmar: Standard dialogue formula introducing Job's reply. The verb ʿānāh (to answer, respond) followed by the verb of speech is the LXX's standard rendering throughout the dialogues. Job's reply (Job 6-7) has two distinct movements: an address to the three friends (Job 6:1-30) defending the legitimacy of his complaint, and a direct address to God (Job 7:1-21) that is essentially a lament psalm. The shift in addressee (human interlocutors → God) is a key structural feature of the dialogue sections.
2: lû-šāqôl yiššāqēl kaʿaśî wəhawwāṯî ḇammōʾznayim yiśśāʾû yaḥaḏ: 'Oh that my vexation were weighed, and all my calamity laid in the balances.' The weighing image (šāqal, to weigh; mōʾznayim, scales/balances) invokes the Egyptian judgment scene but also the wisdom tradition of measuring evidence carefully. Kaʿaś (vexation, grief, provocation) is the same word Eliphaz used in 5:2 as the emotion that kills the fool — Job takes that word and proposes to weigh it objectively. Hawwāh (calamity, ruin) is a strong term (cf. Psalms 5:9; Job 52:2; Job 55:11; Proverbs 17:4). Job's argument: if his distress were actually measured, the friends would understand why his words have been extreme.
3: kî-ʿattāh mîḥôl yammîm yiḵbād ʿal-kēn ḏəḇāray lāʿû: 'For then it would be heavier than the sand of the sea; therefore my words have been rash.' The comparison to sand (ḥôl yammîm, sand of the sea) invokes the Abrahamic innumerable-descendants image but reverses it: Job's suffering outweighs even the immeasurable. Lāʿû (rash, impetuous — from lûaʿ, to swallow/gulp) is a self-critique: Job admits his words have been hasty. This is not a retraction of their content (he will maintain his innocence throughout) but an acknowledgment that extreme suffering produces extreme speech — and that extreme speech deserves the weight of the suffering that produced it, not the dismissal Eliphaz offered.
4: kî ḥiṣṣê šadday ʿimmāḏî ʾăšer ḥămaṯām šōṯāh rûḥî biʿûṯê ʾělôah yaʿărəḵûnnî: 'For the arrows of the Almighty are in me; my spirit drinks their poison; the terrors of God are arrayed against me.' This is one of Job's most intense images. Ḥiṣṣê šadday (arrows of the Almighty) is warrior-deity imagery: God as divine archer whose arrows have found their mark in Job. The image of ḥamāh (poison/venom) drunk by the spirit extends the metaphor — these are envenomed arrows. Biʿûṯê ʾělôah (terrors of God) uses the rare biʿûṯāh (a terrifying event, hapax) for divine assault. Job is not complaining about natural illness but about experienced divine aggression — a theological claim of the highest seriousness. The friends interpret suffering as consequence of sin; Job experiences it as direct divine action, which he cannot reconcile with his innocence.
5: hăyinʿaq-pereʾ ʿălê-ḏešeʾ ʾim-yigʿeh-šôr ʿal-bəlîlô: 'Does the wild donkey bray when he has grass, or the ox low over his fodder?' The first of two animal analogies defending the naturalness of complaint under extreme conditions. The wild ass (pereʾ) only brays when it lacks grass; the ox only lows when its feed (bəlîl, mixed fodder) is absent. Job's argument: my cries are not complaints without cause — they are the natural sound of an animal in genuine distress. The analogy disarms the friend's criticism by appealing to observable animal behavior: complaint is evidence of need, not of character deficiency. This is a more honest account of lament than Eliphaz's formula allows.
6: hăyēʾāḵēl ṯāp̄ēl mibbəlî-mālaḥ ʾim-yēš-ṭaʿam bəriyr ḥallāmûṯ: 'Can that which is tasteless be eaten without salt, or is there any taste in the juice of the mallow?' The second food analogy shifts from animal hunger to human appetite. Ṯāp̄ēl (insipid, tasteless, a hapax) describes food that cannot be eaten without mālaḥ (salt), the standard preservative and flavor-agent of the ancient world. Ḥallāmûṯ (mallow/purslane) is another hapax; its juice was considered medicinal but bitter. Job's point: just as tasteless food requires salt to be acceptable, his extreme words are the natural seasoning of unbearable suffering. He is not apologizing for his speech but contextualizing it.
7: mēʾănāh lānəgôaʿ nap̄šî hēmmāh kidwê laḥmî: 'My appetite refuses to touch them; they are as food that is loathsome to me.' Nep̄eš (here rendered 'appetite,' but also soul/self/desire) refuses (māʾan) the friends' counsel because it is loathsome (dōweh, sick, faint, also used of menstrual illness, Leviticus 15:33, Lamentations 1:13 — a term of visceral revulsion). The verse connects back to the food imagery of Job 6:6-7: just as tasteless food is unpalatable, the friends' words are nauseating in their inadequacy. The self is not merely intellectually disagreeing with the friends but experiencing their counsel as physically repellent — a sign of the depth of his suffering and their failure to reach him.
8: mî-yiṯṯēn ṯāḇôʾ šeʾělāṯî wəṯiqwāṯî yiṯṭēn ʾělôah: 'Oh that I might have my request, and that God would fulfill my hope.' The mî-yiṯṯēn (who will grant? / oh that!) formula is Job's characteristic expression of desperate longing (cf. Job 11:5; Job 13:5; Job 14:4, 13; Job 19:23; Job 23:3; Job 29:2). It signals a wish for something that seems impossible given the current situation. Šeʾělāh (request, petition) and tiqwāh (hope, expectation) pair the specific petition with the underlying orientation of faith: Job still expects something from God, even in extremity. His hope (tiqwāh) is not yet extinguished — which is why the specific content of the wish (death, Job 6:9) is so stark.
9: wəyōʾēl ʾělôah wîdakkəʾēnî yatṭēr yāḏô wîḇaṣṣəʿēnî: 'that it would please God to crush me, that he would let loose his hand and cut me off.' The wish is for death by divine action: dāḵāʾ (to crush, oppress; the same root as the children 'crushed in the gate' in Job 5:4) and bāṣaʿ (to cut off/through, possibly from weaving: to cut the thread of life). Job does not wish for suicide but for God to complete what he has begun: if YHWH is against him, let the divine hand act decisively rather than prolonging the anguish. This is not despair without faith — it is a theodicy challenge: if God is going to crush me, do it; the prolonged suffering is the unbearable part.
10: wəṯəhî ʿôḏ niḥāmāṯî waʾăsallēḏāh bəḥîlāh lōʾ-yaḥmôl kî-lōʾ kiḥaḏtî ʾimrê qāḏôš: 'This would be my comfort; I would even exult in pain unsparing, for I have not denied the words of the Holy One.' Remarkable: death would be Job's comfort (niḥāmāh, consolation). But the grounds are theologically significant: he has not denied (kāḥaḏ, to hide, deny, suppress) the words of the Holy One (ʾimrê qāḏôš). Qāḏôš as a divine epithet is relatively rare in Job and occurs here as a term of absolute divine holiness. Job's innocence is grounded not in moral self-righteousness but in fidelity to divine speech: he has not suppressed or denied God's word. This is the book's central claim: suffering does not require either denial of God or denial of one's integrity.
11: māh-kōḥî kî-ʾăyaḥēl ûmah-qiṣṣî kî-ʾaʾărîḵ nap̄šî: 'What is my strength, that I should wait? And what is my end, that I should be patient?' The rhetorical questions confront the friends' demand for patience with physical and existential realism. Kōaḥ (strength, power) and qēṣ (end, limit) define the parameters of endurance: how much strength does a man have, and what end justifies the suffering? The verb yāḥal (to hope, wait) is the standard wisdom-psalm term for patient trust (Psalms 27:14; Job 31:24; Job 33:18, 20, 22; Job 130:5, 7). Job is not rejecting hope in God but challenging the friends' easy prescription of patience by asking: what does patience look like when strength is gone?
12: ʾim-kōḥ ʾăḇānîm kōḥî ʾim-bəśārî nāḥûš: 'Is my strength the strength of stones, or is my flesh bronze?' The rhetorical answer is obvious: no. ʾăḇānîm (stones) and nāḥûš (bronze/copper) represent indestructible materials — the human body is neither. The question exposes the unrealism of the friends' counsel: they prescribe endurance for a body that is flesh and blood, not stone and bronze. The image will recur in the Servant Songs: 'I have set my face like a flint' (Isaiah 50:7), where resolution in suffering requires divine strengthening precisely because human flesh is not stone.
13: hăʾim ʾên ʿezrāṯî ḇî wəṯûšiyyāh niḏḏəḥāh mimmennî: 'Have I any help in me, when resource is driven from me?' ʿEzrāh (help, resource within oneself) and tûšiyyāh (abiding success/sound wisdom, the same wisdom term as in Job 5:12) are both absent. Job is not feigning weakness as a rhetorical move — he is diagnosing his actual condition: all inner and outer resources have been expelled (nāḏaḥ, driven away). The verse bridges from Job's self-assessment (Job 6:11-12) to his indictment of the friends (Job 6:14ff): when a man has no internal resource left, what he needs is ḥeseḏ from a friend, not reproof.
14: lammās mērēʿēhû ḥāseḏ wəyirʾaṯ šadday yaʿăzôḇ: 'He who withholds kindness from a friend forsakes the fear of the Almighty.' One of Job's most theologically dense verses. Ḥeseḏ (covenant lovingkindness, steadfast loyalty) is the defining term of YHWH's covenant character (Exodus 34:6-7) and is here claimed as an obligation owed between friends. To withhold ḥeseḏ from a mās (one who is dissolving/despairing — the participle of māsas, to dissolve, melt) is to ʿāzaḇ (forsake) the fear of the Almighty: cruelty to a suffering friend is tantamount to apostasy. The verse inverts the friends' logic: they claimed Job's suffering proves his sin; Job claims the friends' failure of ḥeseḏ proves their unfaithfulness. The NT fulfillment: Christ's ḥeseḏ toward the suffering is the embodiment of this verse (Matthew 11:28-29).
15: ʾaḥay ḇāgəḏû kəmô-nāḥal kiʾăp̄îqê nəḥālîm yaʿăḇōrû: 'My brothers are treacherous as a torrent-bed, as torrential streams that pass away.' The wadi metaphor begins: nāḥal (wadi, seasonal stream channel) and ʾāp̄îq (water channel, river-bed) are terms for the wadis of the Levant and Sinai — dry beds that flood dramatically in rainy season and run completely dry in summer. The simile indicts the friends for the most fundamental form of relational betrayal: they present themselves as reliable support but, like wadis, are present only when conditions are favorable. Bāgaḏ (treacherous, to deal deceitfully) is the term used for covenant betrayal (Jeremiah 3:20; Hosea 5:7; Malachi 2:10, 11, 14, 15, 16).
16: haqqōḏərîm miqqāraḥ ʿălêhem yiṯʿallam-šāleḡ: 'which are dark with ice, and where the snow hides itself.' The wadi in winter/early spring: black with melted snow and hidden ice (qāraḥ, ice/frost). The streams appear full and reliable — but this is temporary, as Job 6:17-20 explain. The word yiṯʿallam (to hide itself, conceal) will recur in Job's speeches in the context of God's hiddenness (Job 13:24: 'Why do you hide your face?'). The wadi that hides its snow/reliability anticipates God's hiddenness as another form of the friends' unreliability — or perhaps vice versa.
17: bəʿēṯ yəzōrəbû niṣmāṯû bəḥummô dāʿăkû mimməqômām: 'When they melt, they disappear; when it is hot, they vanish from their place.' The summer reversal: zāraḇ (to scorch, cause to vanish through heat) and dāʿaḵ (to be quenched, extinguished — used of lamplight going out, Proverbs 13:9; Job 20:20) describe the wadi's summer disappearance. The very heat that travelers hope will make a stream more accessible dries it completely. The theology of the friends as wadis: they appeared reliable in comfortable times; Job's extreme suffering is the summer heat that revealed their absence. Real friendship, like real ḥeseḏ, must survive the heat.
18: yiləpəṯû ʾorḥôṯ darkām yaʿălû ḇattōhû wayyōʾḇēḏû: 'The caravans of Tema look, the travelers of Sheba hope.' Wait — this verse reads: 'the caravans turn aside from their paths; they go up into the waste and perish.' The travelers — not yet mentioned — are arriving at a wadi that has gone dry. Ṯēmāʾ (Tema) is the Arabian trading station (Job 1:15; Isaiah 21:14; Jeremiah 25:23) and Šəḇāʾ (Sheba) is the South Arabian trading kingdom (Job 1:15; 1 Kings 10:1-13): both are trading routes dependent on water sources. When the expected wadi is dry, the caravan must abandon its planned route and perish in the desert (tōhû, the wasteland — the same word as Genesis 1:2's formless void).
19: ḥăḇallôṯ ṯēmāʾ hibbiṭû ʾorḥôṯ šəḇāʾ qiwwû-lāmô: 'They are ashamed because they were confident; they come there and are disappointed.' The merchants' confidence (bāṭaḥ, to trust, rely on) turns to ḇōšāh (shame, disappointment) — the covenantal vocabulary of trust betrayed. The wadi that promised water delivered nothing. The traveler's expectation (qāwāh, to wait/hope/expect) was false. Job applies this directly to his own experience of the friends: he came to them in distress, expecting the ḥeseḏ that friends owe; he received reproof instead. His disappointment is not petulance but the deep disorientation of betrayed trust.
20: kî-yāḵēlû bāṭaḥ bāhū wîʾātā-ʿāḏêhā wayḇōšû: 'For you have now become nothing; you see my calamity and are afraid.' The application becomes explicit: ʾattāh (you — plural, addressing the friends directly) are nothing (lōʾ-hāyāh in Job 6:21 makes this clearer). The verb yārēʾ (to be afraid) suggests that the friends are frightened by Job's calamity: his radical suffering threatens their retribution theology. If Job suffers, and Job is innocent, then the theology that suffering = sin is false — and their own security is threatened. The friends' unwillingness to offer ḥeseḏ may be partly defensive: to defend Job's innocence would require dismantling their own framework.
21: kî-ʿattāh hĕyîṯem lōʾ tirʾû ḥāṯaṯ wayyîrāʾû: 'Such you have become to me; you see my terror, and you are afraid.' The final diagnosis: the friends have become (hāyāh lĕ-) to Job what the dry wadi is to the caravan — nothing where there should have been water. Their fear (ḥāṯaṯ, dismay/shattered confidence; yārēʾ, afraid) is the response of men whose theological system is destabilized by Job's case. Job's 'terror' (ḥittat) is the very extremity that should have activated their ḥeseḏ — instead it activated their self-protective theology.
22: hăkî ʾāmartî hāḇû-lî wûmiḵōḥăkem šiḥăḏû ḇaʿăḏî: 'Have I said, "Make me a gift"? Or, "From your wealth offer a bribe for me"?' Job's forensic challenge: he is not asking for money (maṯṯān, a gift/bribe) or intercession through payment (šōḥad, a bribe/present). He wants something more costly: honesty. The introduction of bribery vocabulary (šōḥad) is significant — Job is not manipulating the friends for financial advantage; he is asking for the ḥeseḏ that costs nothing and yet is beyond their capacity to give: recognition of his innocence.
23: wûmiyyadꞌ-ṣar palṭûnî ûmiyyad ʿārîṣîm tipdûnî: 'Or, "Deliver me from the adversary's hand"? Or, "Redeem me from the hand of the ruthless"?' Job's forensic defense continues: he has asked for no material favor, no political intercession, no rescue from enemies. The vocabulary of rescue (pālaṭ, to deliver; pāḏāh, to ransom/redeem) would normally describe prayers for divine deliverance — Job applies it to what he could have asked of the friends but didn't. His actual request is simpler and harder: tell me where I went wrong.
24: hôrûnî waʾănî ʾaḥărîš ûmah-šāḡîṯî hāḇînû lî: 'Teach me, and I will be silent; make me understand how I have gone astray.' A genuine opening: Job commits to silence and learning if the friends can demonstrate his specific sin. Šāgāh (to wander astray, to err) is the verb for inadvertent moral error (Numbers 15:22; Psalms 119:21, 118) rather than deliberate rebellion. Job is not claiming absolute sinlessness — he is claiming he is not aware of the specific sin that could account for his suffering. The offer of silence (ḥārîš) in exchange for evidence is a forensic proposition: show me the evidence, and I will concede. The friends never accept this challenge on its terms.
25: māh-niməṣû diḇrê-yōšer ûmāh-yôkîaḥ hôkēaḥ mikkkem: 'How forceful are upright words! But what does reproof from you reprove?' Niməṣû (are powerful, from māraṣ — to be sharp, strong, forceful) is used only a few times in Job (Job 16:3; 1 Kings 2:8). Upright (yōšer) words have genuine force; but the friends' reproof (hôkîaḥ, the same verb as disciplinary reproof in Job 5:17) has no power because it is not upright. The paradox: the friends claim to be offering true wisdom (as Eliphaz presented it in 5:27 as searched-out truth), but Job claims their reproof fails to reprove because it does not match his actual case.
26: halhôkîaḥ millîm taḥšōḇû wələrûaḥ ʾimrê nōʾāš: 'Do you think that you can reprove words, when the speech of a despairing man is wind?' The verbal reproof of a nōʾāš (despairing one, from yāʾaš — to despair, be without hope) is like criticizing wind: the wind cannot help its direction. Rûaḥ (wind/spirit/breath) here means the naturally uncontrolled outflow of despair. Job's defense of his extreme language: to reprove the speech of a despairing man is to miss the point — the speech is evidence, not sin; the despair requires comfort, not correction. The same point John Calvin makes in his commentary: 'It is unreasonable to require from the sick the same moderation as from those who are well.'
27: ʾap̄-ʿal-yāṯôm tappîlû wətiḵrû ʿal-rēʿăkem: 'You would even cast lots over the fatherless, and bargain over your friend.' The accusation escalates to its sharpest point: the friends are behaving like those who gamble over a fatherless child's inheritance (yāṯôm, orphan — the most vulnerable category in OT law) and make commercial transactions over their friend (kārāh, to dig/trade/bargain). The image of casting lots over the helpless will be fulfilled with horrific literalism at the crucifixion (Psalms 22:18; John 19:24: 'they divided my garments among them and for my clothing they cast lots'). Job, stripped of everything, is being treated like the destitute — by his own friends.
28: wəʿattāh hôʾîlû p̄ənû-ḇî wəʿal-p̄ənêkem ʾim-ʾăḵazzēḇ: 'But now, be pleased to look at me, for I will not lie to your face.' The appeal to direct confrontation: p̄ānāh (to turn toward, face) and ʾim-ʾăḵazzēḇ (I will not lie, the conditional absolute oath). Job invites the friends to look at him directly — not through their theological framework — and commits his own speech to truthfulness. The contrast with the friend's wadi-like unreliability (Job 6:15) is pointed: Job remains transparent even when they do not. The appeal for direct eye contact is a demand for person-to-person engagement over abstract theological dispute.
29: šûḇû-nāʾ ʾal-tehî ʿawlāh wəšûḇû ʿôḏ ṣiḏqî-ḇāh: 'Please turn; let no injustice be done. Turn now; my vindication is at stake.' The repeated šûḇ (turn back, return) is the standard repentance/return verb — here Job uses it against the friends: they need to repent of their false accusation. ʿawlāh (injustice, perversity) is what is being done to Job through false verdicts. Ṣiḏqî-ḇāh (my righteousness is still there/in it) is a claim that Job's integrity is at stake in this dispute — not just his feelings but his covenantal standing before God. The forensic register is fully deployed: Job is not merely complaining, he is making a legal claim.
30: hăyēš-bəlāšônî ʿawlāh ʾim-ḥikkî lōʾ-yāḇîn hawwôṯ: 'Is there any injustice on my tongue? Cannot my palate discern calamity?' Job closes his address to the friends with a self-appeal to sensory discernment: his tongue (lāšôn) is not producing ʿawlāh (injustice/perversity) and his palate (ḥēḵ) can distinguish hawwôṯ (calamity, a strong word for ruin — same root as Job 6:2's 'calamity'). The taste metaphor connects back to the food imagery of Job 6:5-7: just as the palate can distinguish between food that is wholesome and food that is corrupt, Job's palate can distinguish between genuine calamity and imagined complaint. His speech is not corrupt; it is the accurate report of what a discerning palate has tasted.
